nodes.label: Jacques Cornillon's Paris vacuum experiment (reportedly ~10⁻⁶ torr) with Brown's asymmetric capacito
nodes.summary: Jacques Cornillon's Paris vacuum experiment (reportedly ~10⁻⁶ torr) with Brown's asymmetric capacitors showed thrust that residual-ion-wind cannot explain — countering ionic-wind explanations of Biefield-Brown.

sources.quote: Evidence that the Biefield-Brown effect does in a vacuum of at least 10⁻⁶ torr — the residual ambient ionization can't account for that.

type_specific.statement_text: Jacques Cornillon's Paris vacuum experiment (reportedly ~10⁻⁶ torr) with Brown's asymmetric capacitors showed thrust that residual-ion-wind cannot explain — countering ionic-wind explanations of Biefield-Brown.

nodes.label: Herman Bondi's 1957 negative-mass paper produces a solution where a negative-mass object moves towar
nodes.summary: Herman Bondi's 1957 negative-mass paper produces a solution where a negative-mass object moves toward a positive-mass object with unbounded acceleration — eerily matching Brown's asymmetric-capacitor observations.

sources.quote: If you have two masses in general they would accelerate. What if somehow you had a different kind of negative and positive charges oddly the negative and positive mass. You get this weird solution where the negative moves toward the positive like unbounded acceleration.

Townsend Brown is said to have been sent into Nazi Germany in 1945 to retrieve exotic wunderwaffe research, and thereafter closely worked with Robert Sarbacher and Edward Teller.
Robert Sarbacher, physicist, admitted near the end of his life that statements surrounding UFO crashes are 'substantially correct.'
